name: composition_advisor
purpose: JSON knowledge base + decision rules consulted before generating MIDI patterns. Encodes music theory primitives, genre-specific conventions, energy/frequency-space allocations, silence protocols, and conditional 'good->WOW' branches drawn from observed failure modes of prior attempts (Faust, Nemesis, Bramure, Venin).

core_principles.rule: A drop only hits if the bar before STARVES the ear. Build hunger before payoff.
core_principles.concrete_test: Last bar before Drop must have ≥ 1 element (drum, bass, or harmony) drop out OR thin to single track for ≥ 2 beats.

core_principles.rule: Silence is the 4th compositional dimension after pitch/duration/timbre. Plant it deliberately, not by omission.
core_principles.concrete_test: Every section ≥ 8 bars contains at least one PLANNED silence (sub-bar to multi-bar) at a structural pivot.

core_principles.rule: A Final Drop is more memorable when it RESTATES something from earlier (motif, single note, rhythmic idea) than when it adds new layers.
core_principles.concrete_test: Final Drop must contain ≥ 1 callback (intro_drone, verse_motif octave-up, bridge_chord_resolution, or rhythmic quote).

core_principles.rule: Each section carries exactly ONE compositional idea (rhythmic OR melodic OR harmonic OR textural). Stacking ideas crowds; isolating one makes it audible.
core_principles.concrete_test: Identify each section's PRIMARY idea in 1 sentence. If multiple compete, simplify.

core_principles.rule: When ≥ 2 instruments share a critical band, exactly ONE leads; the other yields (-6 dB OR pan ≥ 40% off-center OR sidechain duck).
core_principles.concrete_test: Audit by band: at most 1 dominant per [sub, low, low-mid, mid, high-mid, high, air].

core_principles.rule: Velocity curves > velocity steps. Timing offsets ±1/64 humanize. Pitch wobble (±10 cents) adds desperate/menacing quality. Mathematically perfect = sterile.
core_principles.concrete_test: No pattern should use a single velocity value across all hits of one instrument in one section.

philosophy.good_to_wow_meta: Each conditional branch in this API encodes ONE specific lift from 'sounds OK' to 'memorable'. Apply branches conservatively — one well-executed WOW per section beats five competing ones.

voicing_recipes.i_sus2: root + 2 + 5 — leaves the b3 implicit, dark but unresolved
voicing_recipes.VI_open: b6 + 1 + b3 — wide open, classic Aeolian
voicing_recipes.VII_to_i: VII major resolves down a whole step to i — the Aeolian cadence

aeolian.characteristic_dissonance: VII_to_i (Aeolian cadence is itself the tension — feels modal, not Classical-tonal)

chord_families.ii: minor (the natural 6 lives here as the chord's 5)
chord_families.IV: major (b3, 6, 1) — the SIGNATURE Dorian chord, lifted by raised-6

voicing_recipes.i_to_IV: the i->IV move is the Dorian payoff — the raised 6 is suddenly audible
voicing_recipes.i_add6: root + b3 + 5 + 6 — Dorian without committing to chord IV

use_for: verse that wants relief without resolution
dorian.characteristic_dissonance: natural 6 against b3 (a major 4th interval that shouldn't exist in pure minor) — the Dorian raised-6 tension

signature_intervals: b2 (the half-step ABOVE the root — primary tension)

chord_families.bII: major (b2, 4, b6) — the SIGNATURE Phrygian chord, tritone-laden push from i

voicing_recipes.i_with_b9: root + b3 + 5 + b9 (the b2 pitched up an octave) — root + immediate dissonance
voicing_recipes.i_to_bII: the half-step UP move from i to bII is the Phrygian signature — sounds Spanish/flamenco/menacing
voicing_recipes.i_sus_b2: root + b2 + 5 — sus voicing where the b2 is the suspension

phrygian.characteristic_dissonance: b2 against root = minor 2nd, the primary musical 'wrongness' interval. Use sparingly or mode loses identity.

phrygian_dominant.scale_name: Phrygian Dominant (5th mode of harmonic minor)
signature_intervals: MAJOR 3 (the maj-3 over b2 = aug-2 interval = snake-charmer)

chord_families.I: major (1, 3, 5) — surprise major chord on the dark mode

voicing_recipes.I_dominant7_no5: root + maj3 + b7 — the venom voicing, no 5 lets the maj3-vs-b7 tritone dominate
voicing_recipes.I_sus4: root + 4 + 5 — avoid committing the maj-3 (lets bass deliver it)

phrygian_dominant.characteristic_dissonance: aug-2 interval between b2 and maj-3 (D to F nat in C# Phr Dom) — the unmistakable signature.

signature_intervals: b5 (the diminished 5th — root chord is i°, NO stable consonance)

chord_families.i_dim: diminished — the root chord itself is unstable

voicing_recipes.i_dim_no5: root + b3 — pseudo-stable by hiding the b5
voicing_recipes.i_with_b5: root + b3 + b5 — full diminished, maximum instability

locrian.characteristic_dissonance: Locrian has NO stable tonic — the root is itself a tritone away from the b5. Treat as a passing flavor, not a home key. Used wholesale = listener loses anchor.
locrian.warning: Per user preference (less dissonance), prefer Phrygian or Phrygian Dominant instead of Locrian for most use cases.

signature_intervals: natural 7 (leading tone — this is what makes it 'harmonic')

chord_families.V: MAJOR (5, 7, 2) — the SIGNATURE chord, contains the leading tone — Classical V-i cadence

voicing_recipes.V_to_i: the dominant V to tonic i resolution — feels classical but with the b6 lurking
voicing_recipes.i_add_nat7: root + b3 + 5 + nat7 — minor-major 7 chord, very brooding
voicing_recipes.iv_to_V_to_i: ii->V->i but with both ii dim and V major — the harmonic minor cadence

avoid_for: modal (use Aeolian/Dorian instead) — Harmonic Minor pulls toward classical resolution
harmonic_minor.characteristic_dissonance: the aug-2 between b6 and nat-7 — exoticism that resolves cleanly via the leading tone.

voice_leading.purpose: How voices (instruments treated as melodic lines) move in relation to each other. Modern grunge/industrial breaks classical rules deliberately — these rules document both the conventions AND the genre exceptions, so the advisor can grant exception permits with intent.

core_rules.rule: Inner voices (pad chord tones, counter-leads, sustained synths) should move by step (m2 or M2) when possible. Leaps > minor 3rd in inner voices sound jarring.

exceptions.reason: octave displacement on bass = power move, not voice-leading violation
lead jumping octave UP at section peak amplifies climax

core_rules.rule: Two voices moving in PARALLEL 5ths or octaves between chord changes sounds 'thin' or 'primitive' in classical writing.

exceptions.reason: Cobain genre — root+5 IS the chord, parallel motion is the GENRE
doubling the lead in octaves is standard production
modern modal voicings move in 4ths/5ths intentionally

core_rules.rule: When changing chords, keep any shared note in the SAME voice (don't move to a different instrument). Reduces motion, preserves continuity.
core_rules.wow_lift: smoothest chord changes the listener barely perceives — 'glue'

core_rules.rule: When outer voices move opposite directions (bass UP while top voice DOWN, or inverse), the harmony feels grounded and inevitable.
core_rules.wow_lift: cadential motion sounds like resolution even when modal

core_rules.rule: In Harmonic Minor, the natural 7 (leading tone) MUST resolve UP to root within 2 beats. Otherwise the cadence breaks.
core_rules.violation_penalty: loss of cadential pull — chord progression sounds aimless

core_rules.rule: Drone voices NEVER move within a section. If a drone must change, do it AT THE SECTION BOUNDARY by sliding a half-step (chromatic shift, no bigger).
core_rules.wow_lift: the half-step drone shift at a boundary signals 'something has changed' subliminally

between_instruments.bass_vs_pad: Bass moves while pad holds (or inverse) — never both move on same beat except at section change. Reduces clash, preserves clarity.
between_instruments.lead_vs_counter_lead: If lead moves UP by step, counter-lead moves DOWN by step (contrary motion = independence). If both move parallel, they fuse into one voice.
between_instruments.drone_role: Drone is the SPINE — every other voice references it. Moving voices should land on drone-compatible chord tones at downbeats.
between_instruments.kick_vs_sub_bass: Kick LOCKS pitch with sub-bass on downbeats. Sub-bass holds pitch BETWEEN kicks (no clash). Ducking sub-bass on kick attack is OPTIONAL but recommended for clarity.
between_instruments.vocal_vs_lead_synth: Vocal occupies 200-3kHz primarily. If lead synth shares this band, lead synth must drop -3 dB OR pan ≥ 30% off-center when vocal is present.

anti_patterns_voice_leading: All voices moving in parallel motion → sounds like one instrument doubled
Bass and pad both moving on every beat → muddy, no anchor
Lead and counter-lead doing the same melodic shape an interval apart → no independence, defeats the counter-lead purpose
Drone changing pitch mid-section → breaks the spine

tension_release.purpose: Cadences and tension techniques — how to build expectation and either deliver or subvert it. The 'WOW' lift comes from CHOOSING when to resolve vs surprise.

use_for: Final Drop landing if home key
modes: harmonic_minor (only modal cadence with strong V)
authentic.comment: Sounds 'classical' / safe. Reach for this when you want unmistakable arrival.

modal_aeolian.form: bVII major -> i minor (descending whole step)

deceptive.form: V (build expectation of i) -> VI major (instead)
deceptive.strength: surprise twist — listener expects resolution, gets a major lift somewhere new
use_for: bridge ending that DOESN'T resolve to home
modulation pivot via the unexpected chord
deceptive.comment: Most underused powerful cadence. Use ONCE per song max.

interrupted_silence.form: build chord -> SILENCE (1 to 2 beats) -> resolution chord
interrupted_silence.comment: Combines tension_release WITH silence_protocol — the gap IS the cadence

suspended_unresolved.form: end on sus2 or sus4 (or open quartal voicing), never resolve to triad
use_for: modal hover that intentionally stays unfinished
suspended_unresolved.comment: Anti-classical — leaves the listener thinking the song could continue forever

plagal_modal.form: iv -> i (subdominant -> tonic, no V involvement)
use_for: after a deceptive cadence to soothe

neapolitan.definition: bII major chord built on flat 2 in a minor key (e.g. Db major in C minor)
neapolitan.function: Subdominant substitute with chromatic flavor — in 1st inversion (bII6) is classic, in root position is more modern.
neapolitan.example_in_C_minor: Db major chord — feels like a doorway

chromatic_mediant.definition: Sudden chord shift to a 3rd-related root (bIII, III, bVI, VI) without prep — feels surprising because no common-tone setup.
chromatic_mediant.function: Instant mood pivot WITHOUT modulation (returns to home key after).
chromatic_mediant.comment: Reznor / Yorke signature. The chord drops into the listener like an interruption from another world.

pivot_half_step.definition: Whole arrangement shifts root by minor 2 (up or down). Every voice moves +1 or -1 semitone.
pivot_half_step.function: The 'half-step modulation' shock — most striking in popular music.
use_for: Final Drop ending crash-back DOWN to home
pivot_half_step.duration_recommendation: First 4 bars of the section ONLY, then crash back home; OR sustain the new key to end (commits to it).
pivot_half_step.comment: Used in Nemesis Final Drop. User loved this. Reuse strategically — it loses power if used twice in same song.

tritone_substitution.definition: bII7 replaces V7 — same tritone interval but bass is a half-step above tonic instead of a 5th below.
tritone_substitution.function: Jazz move that ADAPTS to metal — adds chromatic density without losing the V function.
use_for: pre-Drop chord that lands HARD on the Drop
any V-to-i moment that wants more bite

false_relation.definition: Two voices simultaneously play the SAME pitch class natural and altered (e.g. C natural in bass, C# in pad — at the same beat).
false_relation.function: Momentary clash that resolves within 1 beat to stable harmony — Reznor signature dissonance.
use_for: Drop pre-roll for 1 beat of menace
false_relation.comment: Don't sustain — dissonance must clear quickly or it sounds like a mistake.

silence_as_dominant.definition: Replace the V (dominant) chord with literal silence of equivalent length. The expectation of V is so strong that absence ITSELF cadences.
silence_as_dominant.function: Silence borrows the function of V via expectation — Reznor / NIN trick.
use_for: pre-Drop where you'd normally place a sustained V chord
silence_as_dominant.comment: Pairs with interrupted_silence cadence — same idea applied differently

always_resolve: Final Drop entry (the listener earned this)
never_resolve: Outro if Yorke ≥ 25% (ostinato fade > authentic cadence)
Hollow ending (the void has its own logic — resolution would feel cheap)
resolve_with_surprise: Bridge ending → use deceptive cadence (V→VI not V→i)
Drop entry from Pre-Drop → use interrupted_silence (silence + landing)
modulation_resolve: If pivot_half_step UP at Final Drop bars 1-4, resolve back to home with authentic cadence at bar 5
If chromatic_mediant chord introduced, RETURN to home within 4 bars max

anti_patterns_tension: Using authentic cadence at every section boundary → predictable, no impact
Multiple deceptive cadences in same song → loses surprise value
Sustaining false_relation > 1 beat → sounds like a wrong note, not a feature
Modulating up half-step AND down half-step in same song → cancels emotional direction
Silence after silence → silence loses meaning if it's the default

voicings_recipes.purpose: Concrete intervallic recipes for stacking notes. Each recipe has a polyphony cost (n voices), a character description, and pairing rules with other recipes when 2 instruments stack on the same chord. The advisor picks recipes by section context + genre blend, not by name alone.

power_chord.character: wide-open, primal, no commitment to maj/min — root + perfect 5
best_for: any track that wants weight without harmonic specificity
pairs_well_with: drone_over_moving (drone on root, power chord above)

sus2.character: suspended 2 — major-second instead of 3rd, leaves maj/min unresolved
best_for: pad voicings that hold without committing
pairs_well_with: power_chord (sus2 in pad, power in bass)

sus4.character: suspended 4 — perfect 4 instead of 3rd, classical 4-3 suspension when resolved (or held unresolved for modal hover)

drop_2.character: jazz voicing — root, octave, 5, 3 (the 2nd voice from top dropped an octave). Spreads the chord wide.
drop_2.example_in_C_minor: C3 + Eb4 + G3 + C4 (rearranged: C2 + G3 + C3 + Eb3)

quartal.character: built in 4ths instead of 3rds — root + 4 + b7. Modal/modern, no Classical tonal pull
best_for: static harmony that doesn't want to resolve

drone_over_moving.character: sustained low note (drone) with melodic voices above. The drone is pedal point.
best_for: bridge that wants stasis under motion
avoid_for: sections where bass needs to move with chord changes
drone_over_moving.example_in_C_minor: C2 sustained, with C3-Eb3-F3-Eb3 moving above
pairs_well_with: any moving voicing — that's the whole point

first_inversion.character: chord with 3rd in bass — softer than root position, common in voice-led sequences
avoid_for: weight-bearing power moments (root position has more authority)

slash_chord_modal.character: chord with NON-chord-tone bass (e.g., Cm/D) — creates suspended tension
pairs_well_with: any voicing that resolves to home root

min7_no5.character: minor 7 without the 5 — sparse, modern, brooding. Reznor sparse signature.
best_for: verse harmony that wants brood without thickness

open_fifth_dyad.character: JUST root + 5 spread WIDE (root in low octave, 5 nearly 2 octaves up). Maximum width, minimum density.
pairs_well_with: drone_over_moving (the open fifth IS the spine)

vocal_octave_double.character: vocal + lead synth on SAME melody, octave apart — fuses two voices into 'bigger version of vocal'
avoid_for: sections where vocal needs to be exposed alone
vocal_octave_double.example_in_C_minor: vocal at C4, lead synth at C5 (or C3)

chromatic_neighbor_pad.character: root + b9 (chromatic neighbor) + 5 — micro-dissonance held briefly. Reznor false-relation setup.
avoid_for: sustained sections (the b9 will fatigue the ear)
pairs_well_with: resolution to power_chord on next bar
chromatic_neighbor_pad.warning: do NOT sustain >1 bar — sounds like a wrong note instead of a feature

stacking_rules_when_two_instruments_play.principle: Two voicings on the same chord MUST have different intervallic structures so they don't double-up the same notes. The exception is intentional unison/octave doubling.

compatible_pairs.result: the maj-2 floats above the open fifth — Cobain dirty pad
Reznor sparse pad under doubled vocal
Yorke modal hover with pedal bass
lush jazz pad under exposed voice

silence_protocol.purpose: Silence as the 4TH compositional dimension after pitch/duration/timbre. Each level has a wow_factor and explicit placement rules. Silence + cadence pairs are documented because the BIGGEST WOW moments combine both.
silence_protocol.philosophy: Silence is content, not absence. A drop that hits after a planted silence is more memorable than a drop that hits after sound. The advisor should plant silences DELIBERATELY at structural pivots, not by accident.

section_silence.definition: Whole section thinned to 1-2 tracks max — the 'Hollow' section in Venin/Nemesis style. Not literal silence — but everything except 1-2 sustained drone/atmosphere tracks drops out.
placement_rules: Plant ONCE per song — overuse kills its impact
Position right AFTER a major Drop (not before)
The 1-2 surviving tracks must include a sustained drone (continuity reference)
Preceding section ends abruptly (no fade) — the silence IS the transition
section_silence.callback_potential: the surviving drone in section_silence can be CALLED BACK in Final Drop as a fade-in layer (callback through silence)

bar_silence.definition: 1-2 full bars of TOTAL silence in the middle of an otherwise busy section. Most powerful at structural pivots inside drops.
placement_rules: Canonical position: bar 13 of a 16-bar Drop (3/4 through — after enough density to feel the loss)
OR last bar of Pre-Drop into Final Drop (interrupted_silence cadence)
OR bar 7 of an 8-bar Bridge (before the final 8th bar landing)
All tracks cut simultaneously — no fade
The bar AFTER must reenter at maximum density for max impact

beat_silence.definition: 1-2 beats of silence within a bar — usually replacing a hit you'd expect (the listener has been trained on the pattern).
placement_rules: Replace the LAST cluster hit (e.g., 3.75) with silence on EXACTLY 1 bar out of 8 (or 1 out of 4 in shorter sections)
OR replace beat 1 of a bar (the most expected hit) — extreme effect, max 1× per song
Pattern context: must surround the silenced beat with the FULL pattern before and after — the gap must be inside an established pattern
beat_silence.comment: Easy to overdo. If used > 2× per section, the pattern becomes the silence and the silence loses meaning.

subbar_silence.definition: 1/16 to 1/2 beat of silence — micro-gap in a busy 16th-note pattern. Velocity = 0 on specific hits to create breath inside groove.
placement_rules: Apply to hi-hat, arp, or perc patterns — never to kick (kick should always speak)
OR pattern: random 1-in-8 muted for human imperfection
Do NOT mute on downbeats — only on offbeat 16ths
subbar_silence.comment: Subtle — adds groove humanization. NOT a structural surprise but a textural one.

silence_cadence_pairs.principle: The BIGGEST WOW moments combine a tension_release cadence with a silence — the gap IS the cadence's resolution.

pairs.name: interrupted_silence_cadence
pairs.execution: Pre-Drop ends mid-phrase (no resolution). 1 bar of total silence. Final Drop bar 1 entry hits with cluster_density_max.

pairs.name: silence_as_dominant
pairs.context: anywhere a V chord would functionally lead to i
pairs.execution: Replace what would be 2 bars of dominant chord with 2 bars of nothing. The expectation of V is so strong that absence functions as V.

pairs.name: subtle_breath
pairs.execution: After bII chord lands, 1 beat of silence before i resolution. The breath is the 'oh' moment.

pairs.name: groove_breath
pairs.execution: 1-in-8 hi-hat muted across the section. Adds subliminal groove without breaking pattern.

silence_callback_strategy.principle: Silence used in an EARLIER section becomes a callback when used again in a later section.
examples: Hollow (section_silence at midpoint) → fade in Hollow's surviving drone in Final Drop bar 5 → instant emotional payoff
Pre-Drop ends with 1-bar silence → mirror in pre-Final-Drop with 2-bar silence → escalation
Bar-13 silence in Drop 1 → mirror at bar-13 of Final Drop, but EXTENDED to 2 bars → wow

rhythm_theory.purpose: Codifies the rhythmic dimension — polyrhythm, swing, groove feel — so the advisor can pick rhythmic devices that match section energy and genre. Without this, all generated patterns sit on a sterile mathematical grid.

polyrhythm.definition: Two or more rhythmic streams with different subdivisions playing simultaneously. The streams realign every N beats (the LCM of their cycles).

3_against_2.definition: 3 evenly-spaced hits in the time of 2 beats (or 6 against 4). Most common polyrhythm.
3_against_2.feel: loose, flowing, slight tension between the two streams
best_for: any moment that wants to break the grid without breaking the meter
3_against_2.concrete_recipe: if 4/4 bar = 4 beats, place hits at 0, 0.6667, 1.3333, 2.0, 2.6667, 3.3333 (6 hits across 4 beats)

4_against_3.definition: 4 evenly-spaced hits in the time of 3 beats (used over 3/4 or as a hemiola in 4/4)
4_against_3.feel: very destabilizing — the 4 stream feels 'wrong' against 3
best_for: bridge moments that want metric ambiguity
intro polyrhythm to disorient before locking in
4_against_3.concrete_recipe: in 4/4 bar, place 4 evenly across 3 beats: 0, 0.75, 1.5, 2.25 (then beat 3 onwards is a different cycle)
4_against_3.warning: use sparingly — extended use sounds like a meter mistake

5_against_4.definition: 5 hits across 4 beats. Used in Bramure arp; realigns every 5 bars.
5_against_4.feel: shifting groove that drifts in and out of phase with the downbeat
best_for: moments that want continuous motion without locking
5_against_4.concrete_recipe: 5-note cell in 16ths cycles every 1.25 beats; over 4 bars (16 beats) the cell repeats 12.8 times — full realignment every 5 bars

7_against_4.feel: almost arrhythmic, requires anchoring against straight 4/4 to be perceived as polyrhythm not chaos
7_against_4.warning: Yorke would use this; Cobain never. Match to genre_blend before applying.

polyrhythm.anchoring_rule: When using polyrhythm, ALWAYS keep the kick/snare on straight 4/4 grid. The polyrhythm sits OVER the grid — never replace the grid. Without an anchor, polyrhythm becomes noise.

swing_ratios.definition: How a pair of 8th-notes is timed: the offbeat 8th can be exactly halfway (straight) or pushed later (swing).

light_swing.feel: subtle human laid-back, barely perceptible push
best_for: any pattern that should sound 'slightly drunk'

shuffle.warning: Shuffle is rare in industrial/grunge/electronic — apply only if explicitly intended

decision_tree_swing.if_genre_blend_yorke_pct >= 25: prefer light_swing for verse, straight for build/drop
decision_tree_swing.if_section == 'drop' OR section == 'fina: force straight — drops need precision for cluster impact

groove_feel.definition: Where hits land RELATIVE to the metronome. Same notation, different feel.

do_not_apply_to: sub bass — these MUST stay on grid for low-end clarity

behind_beat.feel: laid-back, drag, brooding, heavy — 'sitting in the pocket'

groove_feel.groove_feel_callback: Within a section, varying the groove_feel is a WOW lever — e.g. Verse drags behind-beat, Pre-Drop snaps to on-beat (the sudden tightness telegraphs the drop)

tempo_perception_at_95_bpm.comment: Tempo and feel interact. At 95 BPM (Venin / our preferred slow burn), 8th notes feel 'comfortable' — neither fast nor slow. 16ths feel 'busy'. Triplet 8ths (3-against-2) feel 'loose'.
rules: At 95 BPM, drum hi-hat 16ths sound aggressive (mechanical) — use sparingly
At 95 BPM, drum hi-hat 8ths feel restrained — best default
Triplet feels (6/8 or polyrhythm 3:2) at 95 BPM gravitate toward AIC / Yorke ballad territory
Cluster stabs at 95 BPM hit harder than at 120 — the slow tempo amplifies impact

rhythm_advanced.purpose: Advanced rhythmic devices that ELEVATE a pattern from competent to memorable. Each device is scored by wow_factor and explicit usage rules — overuse breaks the device's effect.

syncopation_typology.definition: Placing accents on weak beats or off-beats, displacing the listener's sense of where the downbeat is.

anticipation_push.definition: An accented hit lands 1/8 or 1/16 EARLIER than expected (push the beat). Listener's expectation of the downbeat creates impact.
anticipation_push.example_at_4_4: snare expected on beat 3 (=2.0) lands at 1.75 instead — 1/16 push
anticipation_push.warning: If everything pushes, nothing pushes. Use on 1-2 specific hits per phrase.

suspension_pull.definition: Accented hit lands 1/8 or 1/16 LATER than expected. Creates tension by withholding.
suspension_pull.example_at_4_4: snare expected on beat 3 lands at 2.25 instead — 1/16 drag

16th_displacement.definition: An entire phrase shifted by 1/16 from its expected grid position. Whole pattern feels off-kilter while remaining metric.
16th_displacement.example_at_4_4: kick pattern that should be [0, 1, 2, 3] becomes [0.25, 1.25, 2.25, 3.25] — same shape, displaced
16th_displacement.warning: Apply to ONE bar then resolve to grid — extended displacement loses meter

and_of_2_push.definition: Cobain signature: snare/vocal accent on the '&' of beat 2 (1.5) instead of the strict beat 2

tresillo_3_3_2.definition: 8th-note pattern grouped 3-3-2 within 4 beats (positions 0, 0.75, 1.5 in 8ths = 3+3+2). The Latin/dance hall building block.
tresillo_3_3_2.example_at_4_4: kick on positions 0, 1.5, 3.0 (8th-note grid steps 0, 3, 6 of 8)
tresillo_3_3_2.feel: natural pulse felt around the world
best_for: any kick pattern that wants forward momentum without 4-on-the-floor

hemiola.definition: Short-duration polyrhythm: 3 groups of 2 against 2 groups of 3 (or vice-versa) within a small span (typically 2-4 bars).

examples.3_groups_of_2_in_3_bars_4_4: in 12 beats, group hits as [4 4 4] (whole notes) instead of [3 3 3 3] — feels like the bar shifted to 3/4 temporarily
examples.2_groups_of_3_in_2_bars_3_4: in 6 beats, group hits as [3 3] instead of [2 2 2]

hemiola.feel: metric ambiguity that resolves — listener almost forgets the meter, then it snaps back
best_for: moments that want to break the grid temporarily
hemiola.warning: Hemiola MUST resolve back to the original meter — if it doesn't, listener loses the meter entirely

breakbeat.definition: Chopping a continuous drum pattern into discrete segments separated by silence or different patterns. Each chop creates a micro-section.

stutter.description: Repeat a tiny segment (1/16 to 1/4 beat) 2-4 times for glitch effect

drum_silence_drop.description: Cut ALL drums for 1-2 beats mid-pattern (often beats 3-4 of bar 4 of a phrase)

snare_only_bar.description: 1 bar where everything cuts except snare (which plays the standard 2/4 backbeat)

kick_only_bar.description: 1 bar where everything cuts except kick (continues the pulse)
best_for: after a section_silence — kick re-establishes the pulse

stop_time.definition: Cut the rhythm section (drums + bass) entirely while keeping melody/harmony moving. Inverts the listener's expectation of who carries pulse.
best_for: bridge moment that wants exposed melody
stop_time.comment: Stop-time is the rhythmic equivalent of false_relation — momentary suspension that resolves
stop_time.callback_strategy: stop_time at end of Verse 1 → callback at start of Final Drop with same melody but FULL rhythm section returns = payoff

subdivision_shifts.definition: Within a section, switching the dominant rhythmic subdivision (8ths -> 16ths -> triplets) — changes the perceived speed without changing tempo

8ths_to_16ths.where: transition from Verse to Pre-Drop (gives sense of acceleration)

16ths_to_triplets.where: Bridge — momentary loose feel before snapping back to straight 16ths in Final Drop

8ths_to_triplets_to_8ths.where: single bar of triplets sandwiched between 8th-note phrases — micro-disorientation

subdivision_halving.description: Suddenly halve the subdivision rate — 16ths drop to 8ths, instant breath

callback_through_rhythm.principle: A specific rhythmic motif (e.g., the and_of_2_push, or a 3-3-2 tresillo) that appears once in an early section CAN be restated in Final Drop for callback effect — even more powerful than a melodic callback because rhythm is felt physically.
examples: Verse 1 has and_of_2_push on snare → Final Drop bar 5 reuses and_of_2_push verbatim → physical recognition
Bridge introduces a 3:2 hemiola → Final Drop closing 2 bars do the same hemiola → 'home stretch' feel

density_curves.purpose: Notes-per-second and simultaneous-voice budgets per section type. The advisor uses these to ENFORCE the 'one idea per section' principle and to calibrate density-as-tension lever (gradual density increase = anticipation; sudden density inversion = WOW).
density_curves.principle_density_vs_intensity: Density (notes/sec) and intensity (perceived energy) are correlated but NOT identical. A Verse can be DENSE but LOW-energy (busy hi-hat + soft chord). A Drop can be SPARSE but HIGH-intensity (single huge cluster stab + silence). Density is a tool, intensity is the goal.

notes_per_second_targets_at_95_bpm.comment: These are SECTION-LEVEL averages, not bar-level. At 95 BPM, 1 beat = 0.632s. A 4/4 bar = 2.53s. An 8th-note pattern = ~3.16 hits/sec.

intro_atmospheric.anti_pattern: more than 6 voices = not an intro anymore

verse_groove.feel: established pulse, room for vocal/lead to breathe

pre_drop_buildup.feel: density INCREASING toward drop entry — riser + drum stutter
pre_drop_buildup.rule: the LAST bar of pre-drop should hit max nps for the section, then DROP to silence or single hit

drop_main.feel: cluster stabs SHARED across 6+ tracks — this counts as ONE rhythmic event, not 6
drop_main.key_insight: Drop nps lower than Pre-Drop because of NEGATIVE SPACE between cluster stabs — the silence between hits is the design

hollow_void.rule: must be UNDER intro nps — otherwise not a true hollow

bridge_modal.rule: lower density than Verse 2 (Bridge SHOULD feel less busy than what came before)

final_drop_fire.feel: highest density of the song BUT every voice has its lane (frequency_surrender enforced)
final_drop_fire.key_insight: Final Drop nps higher than Drop because density itself is part of the climax — but cluster_density of stabs is also higher (8 hits/bar vs 4-5)

outro_decay.feel: thinning, fading, single sustained voice carries last note
outro_decay.rule: must end UNDER intro nps — outro is a mirror of intro but LESS

polyphony_budget.purpose: Hard cap on simultaneous voices per track type — enforces frequency_surrender by reducing collision possibilities.

kick.comment: kick is monophonic — never 2 kicks at same time except cluster sync

snare.comment: monophonic — claps/snares stacked count as 1 event via cluster

drone.comment: single sustained note is the spine

lead_synth.comment: monophonic with octave double for emphasis

vocal.comment: monophonic vocal line — harmonies count as separate vocal_layer track

fx_riser.comment: single sustained pitch, timbre via automation

section_total_polyphony_max.comment: Sum across all active tracks. Exceeding = freq_collision_warning fired by advisor.

density_as_tension_lever.purpose: Density is a PROGRESSIVE lever — the most reliable way to build anticipation without adding new musical content.

gradual_increase.description: Across a 4-bar Pre-Drop, nps grows from 4 to 12. Each bar adds ~2 nps.
gradual_increase.example: Pre-Drop bar 1: kick + sub (4 nps), bar 2: + snare (6 nps), bar 3: + hihat 16ths (10 nps), bar 4: + roll/stutter (14 nps), then Drop entry RESETS to 8 nps with negative space

density_inversion_drop.description: After Pre-Drop max density, Drop entry is SPARSER not denser. The contrast hits harder.
density_inversion_drop.example: Pre-Drop bar 4 = 14 nps stutter, Drop bar 1 = 4 huge cluster stabs (3-4 nps but each stab is 6 tracks aligned)
density_inversion_drop.comment: Counter-intuitive: density DECREASE at the drop = more impact than density INCREASE

negative_space_within_density.description: In a high-density section, plant 1 bar of low-density (subbar_silence on hihat) — listener hears the breath
negative_space_within_density.example: Final Drop bar 13: hihat mutes for 1 bar while everything else continues = listener feels the gap subliminally

callback_through_density.principle: A specific density profile in an early section can be RESTATED later for callback effect.
callback_through_density.example: Verse 1 has a unique density signature (e.g., kick + tom_low + sus pad = 5 nps). Bridge uses this exact density signature mid-section = listener subliminally recognizes 'we were here before'.

anti_patterns_density: Drop denser than Final Drop (Final Drop must be PEAK density)
Hollow denser than Intro (Hollow MUST be the lowest density of the song)
Verse 2 less dense than Verse 1 (when V2 should ADD complexity to V1)
Continuous density from Pre-Drop into Drop (the contrast IS the impact)
All sections at similar density (no shape, no journey)

_TBD_D3: genres (Cobain, Reznor, Yorke, Daft Punk) + per-genre conditional rules
genres (Cobain, Reznor, Yorke, Daft Punk) + per-genre conditional rules

default_personality_blend._note: Sum = 100. More Daft Punk than Yorke per user preference. Each genre's >=25% threshold triggers its conditional rules (D3). NOTE: each axis represents a CLUSTER of related artists (see reference_albums below) — 'cobain_pct' is shorthand for grunge/alt-rock 90s influence as a whole, not just Nirvana.

reference_albums.purpose: User-provided reference albums that anchor the stylistic clusters. Each personality axis (Cobain/Reznor/Yorke/Daft Punk) is a CLUSTER LABEL — the actual influence is broader. The advisor will distill TECHNIQUES (rhythm, harmony, density, sound design choices) from these references during D3 — never specific melodies or lyrics.

Smashing Pumpkins: Mellon Collie and the Infinite Sadness

techniques_to_distill: loud-quiet-loud dynamic shifts (Pixies-derived)
power chord verse / harmonized chorus dynamic
vocal as primary melodic carrier
dropped tunings (drop D / drop C# / drop C)
vocal harmonies in close 3rds and 6ths (AIC signature)
layered guitar takes panned for thickness (Pumpkins signature)
half-time chorus dynamic (QOTSA signature)
swagger groove + behind-beat drag (QOTSA signature)
odd time signatures used sparingly inside 4/4 contexts (Soundgarden — 7/4 verse, 4/4 chorus)
vocal melodic intervals favor minor 3rd / b5 / minor 6th over major intervals
sequenced sparse melodic lines
drone over moving voice as core texture
tape saturation + bit reduction on samples
sudden silence as cadence (silence_as_dominant)
vocal whisper-to-scream contrast within a section
machine-precise drums layered with organic vocal/piano contrast
minor-2nd dissonance as compositional feature, not accident
slow-build atmospheres that erupt without warning
atmospheric noise floor present continuously (FX Tension layer)
modal hover (Aeolian/Dorian without committing to a clear cadence)
vocal ostinato repeating on 1-2 pitches
polyrhythm 3:2 over 4/4 grid
electronic textures placed OVER organic instruments (not under)
descending bass under static melody
lo-fi degradation / band-pass filter as compositional choice
irregular time signatures at structural pivots (5/4 bridge, etc.)
vocal placed low in mix — texture over feature in some sections
song endings that don't resolve (suspended unresolved cadence)
filter sweep automations on lead synths (the riser)
sidechain pump on pad/atmosphere tracks (kick-keyed)
vocoded melodies as melodic carrier
robotic repetition with 5% controlled variation (not random)
modular block arrangements (8-bar phrases that can be rearranged)
live instruments (real bass, real drums) routed through electronic processing
long-form arcs (5-7 min tracks) that earn their length via micro-development
tempo locked, no swing, mechanical precision

cross_cluster_blends_observed_in_referen: NIN With Teeth blends industrial_rock + grunge_alt_rock (heavier guitars, vocal grit)
QOTSA Songs for the Deaf blends grunge + stoner (riff repetition + power chord + half-time chorus)
Radiohead Kid A blends art_rock + electronic (sequenced beats under modal vocals)
Daft Punk RAM blends electronic + retro live-instrument (real drums, real bass)
Smashing Pumpkins Mellon Collie blends grunge + symphonic (orchestrated quiet sections)
reference_albums._will_be_distilled_into: D3a (cobain/reznor genre profiles will draw on grunge_alt_rock_90s + industrial_rock clusters), D3b (yorke/daft_punk profiles), D3c (cross_cluster blend rules)

song_dissection.purpose: Per-song concrete dissection of widely-documented production, structural, and textural choices. Sourced from interviews, production lore, mix-engineer commentary, and AES documentation — NEVER melodic transcription, NEVER lyrics. Each fiche translates into advisor_extract recipes the advisor applies to ORIGINAL compositions inspired by these techniques.
song_dissection.scope_explicit: We dissect: production choices (engineer, room, mic placement when documented), structural blueprints (section architecture, dynamic shifts), sonic textures (drum sound, guitar tone, vocal treatment), arrangement decisions (instrument entry/exit), instrument selection (specific guitars/synths cited in interviews), dynamic shifts (loud-quiet-loud blueprints). We do NOT dissect: specific melodies, lyrics, exact chord voicings as creative expression, or any element that constitutes substantive creative authorship.

primary_producers_cited: Adam Kasper / Eric Valentine (Songs for the Deaf, varies per track)
Josh Homme + Joe Barresi (Lullabies to Paralyze)
_meta.drummers: Dave Grohl on Songs for the Deaf (key signature element); Joey Castellano on Lullabies
_meta.documentation_sources: Homme interviews discussing 'robot rock' philosophy, Grohl drumming clinics, Joe Barresi engineering interviews
_meta.what_distinguishes_them: Repetitive riff-based grooves Homme calls 'robot rock' — riffs that cycle with minimal variation, pulling forward via REPETITION rather than chord changes. 8th-note guitar pulses dominate (downstroke palm-muted patterns). Percussion-forward arrangements with cowbell, tambourine, hand percussion as primary rhythmic elements on some tracks. Half-time chorus dynamics — chorus FEELS slower than verse. Mark Lanegan's gravelly bass vocal adds counterpoint to Homme's higher voice. Songs often earn 5-7 minute lengths via micro-development of repeated material.

No_One_Knows_Songs_For_The_Deaf.structural_blueprint: intro (drum + bass groove establishes — riff isn't introduced yet) -> verse_riff_enters (the iconic syncopated guitar riff joins the groove already in motion) -> chorus (vocal lift, riff continues) -> verse -> chorus -> bridge (different chord area) -> chorus -> outro_riff. The DRUM PATTERN, not the riff, is the song's hook.
signature_production_choices: Dave Grohl's drumming forward in mix — kit feels physically forward
Bass + kick locked tight, drum room mics moderate (not Albini wide, not Wallace dry)
Guitar layered with wide stereo, slight detune between layers
Vocal is mid-mix, not features — the SONG is the groove
No_One_Knows_Songs_For_The_Deaf.rhythmic_signature: Grohl's kick pattern uses syncopated 16th displacement — kick lands on the 'a' (16th note before downbeat) creating push. This pattern became famous and is widely cited.
No_One_Knows_Songs_For_The_Deaf.harmonic_signature: Repetitive riff in minor key — same chord cycle for whole song. The motion is rhythmic, not harmonic.
instrumental_signature: Dave Grohl drums as featured player
No_One_Knows_Songs_For_The_Deaf.the_moment_people_remember: Grohl's drum hook — the kick pattern is so distinctive that the song is often referenced AS that drum pattern. The riff serves the drums, not vice versa.

advisor_extract.description: Make the DRUM pattern the song's primary hook — not the riff or vocal. Build the riff to serve the drum pattern (locking with kick syncopation). Listener's body responds to the rhythm before the harmony.
Kick lands on the '16th-before-downbeat' (the 'a' of the previous beat) instead of the downbeat itself. Creates a forward push that anticipates the bar — listener's body wants to lean forward.
use_for: Verse grooves needing momentum without speed
any pattern wanting forward push without acceleration

advisor_extract.description: Establish drums + bass groove FIRST (4-8 bars). THEN guitar riff enters joining the groove already in motion. Riff feels like an additive layer rather than the song's foundation.
use_for: Intros that center groove over melody

A_Song_For_The_Dead_Songs_For_The_Deaf.structural_blueprint: fast intro (drums + bass + riff at full force) -> verse (sustained intensity) -> chorus_breakdown (HALF-TIME — the chorus is SLOWER than the verse, inverting expectation) -> verse -> chorus_half_time -> long_instrumental_outro (extended jam, 2+ minutes). Half-time chorus contradicts the typical chorus-as-lift template.
signature_production_choices: Drum kit recorded loud, room mics emphasized — Grohl's playing has size
Guitar layers thick — multiple distorted takes panned
Vocal pushed slightly back in mix — drums and guitar are equal/forward
Long instrumental sections allow drums to fully feature
A_Song_For_The_Dead_Songs_For_The_Deaf.rhythmic_signature: Verse: full speed driving 16th hihat with kick + snare backbeat. Chorus: HALVES the perceived tempo — same BPM but kick lands every 2 beats, snare every 4, hi-hat opens up to quarters. The half-time IS the chorus.
A_Song_For_The_Dead_Songs_For_The_Deaf.harmonic_signature: Aeolian/Phrygian flavor minor key, repetitive cycle.
A_Song_For_The_Dead_Songs_For_The_Deaf.the_moment_people_remember: the chorus half-time — the song doesn't speed up, it SLOWS DOWN at the chorus. The contrast between verse drive and chorus weight inverts pop convention.

advisor_extract.description: Chorus HALVES the perceived rhythmic activity (kick on every 2 beats instead of every 1, snare on every 4 instead of every 2). Same BPM, half-time feel. Chorus feels HEAVIER than verse, not lighter.
use_for: Drops that should feel like crashing-down rather than lifting-off

advisor_extract.description: Outro extends 2+ minutes past the last vocal line — pure instrumental jam with band locked on the groove. Listener gets to dwell in the song's identity without being told what to feel.
use_for: songs where the groove is the emotional anchor

Go_With_The_Flow_Songs_For_The_Deaf.structural_blueprint: intro (drums + bass establishing groove) -> verse_riff (single repeated guitar riff, 8th-note pulse) -> chorus (riff continues, vocal lifts) -> verse -> chorus -> bridge (slight chord variation) -> chorus -> outro. Single riff cycles for nearly the entire song — 'robot rock' philosophy at its purest.
signature_production_choices: Single guitar riff repeated 30+ times across the song — micro-variations only
Bass + kick locked perfectly tight
Vocal melody is the variable element on top of the static riff foundation
Go_With_The_Flow_Songs_For_The_Deaf.rhythmic_signature: 8th-note palm-muted guitar pattern (downstrokes) drives the song. Drums lock to this — kick on 1 and 3, snare on 2 and 4 standard. The 8th-note guitar pulse IS the engine.
Go_With_The_Flow_Songs_For_The_Deaf.harmonic_signature: Single repeated riff cycle, minor key — minimal harmonic motion. The song doesn't change chord, it changes texture.
Go_With_The_Flow_Songs_For_The_Deaf.the_moment_people_remember: the relentless repetition — listener gets HOOKED by the way the riff doesn't develop. The mind expects change and is denied it; this becomes the song's mesmeric quality.

advisor_extract.description: Single riff (4-8 bars) repeated for the ENTIRE song with minimal variation. Vocal melody and arrangement layers change ON TOP — but the riff foundation is constant. Mesmeric, hypnotic. The constancy IS the hook.
use_for: Tracks centered on rhythmic/textural identity rather than harmonic motion

advisor_extract.description: Continuous 8th-note palm-muted (or short-staccato) guitar pattern as the song's RHYTHMIC ENGINE. Drums and bass lock to this 8th-note grid. The pulse never stops, creating relentless forward motion.

Little_Sister_Lullabies_To_Paralyze.structural_blueprint: intro (cowbell + minimal drums establish groove — cowbell is THE primary rhythmic element) -> verse (vocal enters over cowbell foundation) -> chorus (slight expansion but cowbell maintains) -> verse -> chorus -> bridge -> chorus -> outro. Cowbell is structural, not ornamental.
signature_production_choices: Cowbell mixed forward — primary rhythmic element, not background
Drums minimal — kick + snare + cowbell + hand percussion. Hi-hat reduced or absent.
Guitar more textural than featured
Vocal mid-mix
Little_Sister_Lullabies_To_Paralyze.rhythmic_signature: Cowbell plays a steady 8th-note pattern. Kick + snare provide 4/4 backbeat over the cowbell pulse.
Little_Sister_Lullabies_To_Paralyze.the_moment_people_remember: the cowbell — it's not a joke or sweetener, it's the song's RHYTHMIC SPINE. Without it, the song doesn't exist.

advisor_extract.description: Make a non-kit percussion element (cowbell, tambourine, woodblock, shaker) THE primary rhythmic spine of the song. Drums fold around it rather than vice versa.
use_for: sections that need a specific TIMBRAL marker

advisor_extract.description: Reduce drum kit to bare minimum (kick + snare + maybe hi-hat). Add 3-4 auxiliary percussion elements (cowbell, tambourine, congas, shaker, claves) to provide rhythmic interest. Density via percussion variety, not kit complexity.
use_for: Verses wanting groove without rock-kit weight

Burn_The_Witch_Lullabies_To_Paralyze.structural_blueprint: intro (clean guitar riff, sparse) -> verse (vocal forward, restrained band) -> chorus (gospel-style female backing vocals enter, full band lifts) -> verse -> chorus -> bridge -> chorus_extended_with_backing_vocals. The gospel backing vocals appearing in chorus are the song's identity-defining choice.
signature_production_choices: GOSPEL-style female backing vocal section in chorus — multiple female singers in close harmony, recorded together
Vocal arrangement style imported from soul/gospel into rock context
Restrained instrumental arrangement — backing vocals get space
Slow blues-rock tempo
Burn_The_Witch_Lullabies_To_Paralyze.rhythmic_signature: Slow 4/4, kick + snare standard backbeat, hi-hat 8ths simple.
Burn_The_Witch_Lullabies_To_Paralyze.harmonic_signature: Bluesy minor with bVII — modal blues feel.
instrumental_signature: clean and slightly distorted guitar layers
GOSPEL FEMALE BACKING VOCAL SECTION (signature element)
Burn_The_Witch_Lullabies_To_Paralyze.the_moment_people_remember: the gospel backing vocals entering on chorus — bringing soul/gospel vocal arrangement INTO a rock track creates immediate genre cross-pollination.

advisor_extract.description: Hire (or sample) a gospel-style backing vocal section (3-4 voices, female, close harmony) and feature them in the chorus of an otherwise rock arrangement. Genre cross-pollination via vocal arrangement.
use_for: songs that benefit from communal voice element

advisor_extract.description: Use a vocal arrangement style from genre A (gospel, soul, opera, choir) inside a track from genre B (rock, electronic, metal). The arrangement TYPE becomes a signal of genre crossover.
use_for: Bridges that depart from song's main genre
Final Drop wanting to elevate beyond rock context

albums_dissected: Mellon Collie and the Infinite Sadness
primary_producers_cited: Billy Corgan as primary arranger across all
_meta.documentation_sources: Butch Vig interviews in Mix Magazine / Tape Op, Corgan interviews, Alan Moulder mix engineering notes
_meta.what_distinguishes_them: EXTREME guitar layering — Corgan would track 4-12 distorted guitar takes panned across the stereo field for chorus walls. Major-minor inversions (verse can be major while chorus is minor or vice versa). Orchestral arrangements integrated with rock band (strings, woodwinds, piano on equal footing with guitars). Wide dynamic range from acoustic intimacy (Disarm) to wall-of-sound choruses (Today). Drum machine experimentation (1979) departed from grunge palette.

Today_Siamese_Dream.structural_blueprint: intro (clean guitar arpeggio in MAJOR key — sounds happy) -> verse (continues major) -> chorus (heavy distortion enters but harmonic content shifts to minor area) -> verse -> chorus -> bridge -> chorus_extended. The MAJOR-to-MINOR shift between verse and chorus is the song's structural signature.
signature_production_choices: Butch Vig recorded multiple guitar layers (Corgan documented playing 4+ takes for chorus walls)
Vocal mostly single-tracked but with subtle delay enrichment
Drum sound clean/dry like Vig's Nevermind work but slightly more roomy
Today_Siamese_Dream.rhythmic_signature: Mid-tempo 4/4, kick + snare standard backbeat, hi-hat 8ths through verse opening up to crash cymbals through chorus.
Today_Siamese_Dream.harmonic_signature: Verse cycles in major key — bright, cheerful sound. Chorus shifts to relative minor / minor area — same key signature but different tonal center. The shift is harmonic, not structural.
instrumental_signature: clean electric guitar arpeggio with chorus pedal
wall of distorted guitars in chorus (4+ tracks panned)
Corgan's nasal vocal as melodic carrier
Today_Siamese_Dream.the_moment_people_remember: the verse sounding HAPPY despite the dark thematic content (the song's lyrical context contradicts the bright sound) — the verse-chorus harmonic inversion creates uncanny mood reversal.

advisor_extract.description: Verse stays in MAJOR key (or major-quality sound). Chorus pivots to relative minor or minor harmonic area. Inverts the typical 'verse minor → chorus major lift' pattern.
use_for: sections that want unsettling emotional shift

advisor_extract.description: Track the SAME chorus guitar part 4-12 separate times, pan across stereo field. Each take's micro-variations create a thick wall of distortion that no single take can match. (In MIDI/sample context: layer 4-6 distorted synth takes with slight detune and timing offsets.)
use_for: Drop or chorus arrangements wanting massive thickness

Mayonaise_Siamese_Dream.structural_blueprint: intro (clean guitar with phaser/chorus modulation — very textural, dreamlike) -> verse (continues clean texture, vocal layered) -> chorus (heavy distortion enters but doesn't displace clean texture — clean and distorted COEXIST stacked) -> verse -> chorus -> instrumental_outro_extended. Layering is the philosophy.
signature_production_choices: Phaser/chorus modulation on clean guitars throughout — wet/dimensional sound
Multiple guitar tracks panned hard left and hard right (Corgan-style stereo wall)
Drum sound mid-bright, snare crisp
Vocal compressed and slightly fuzzy — texture not clarity
Mayonaise_Siamese_Dream.rhythmic_signature: Steady 4/4, mid-tempo. Drums are foundation, not feature.
Mayonaise_Siamese_Dream.harmonic_signature: Modal — 4-chord cycle that doesn't resolve traditionally. Pumpkins specialty.
instrumental_signature: clean guitar with phaser/chorus pedal (very wet)
stacked distorted guitar in chorus that DOESN'T replace clean — adds to it
Mayonaise_Siamese_Dream.the_moment_people_remember: the texture itself — the song doesn't have a 'moment', it has a SOUND. The clean+distorted layering is what people remember, not any specific event.

advisor_extract.description: In chorus, ADD distorted guitar layer ON TOP of clean texture — don't REPLACE clean with distortion. Both palettes coexist. Listener hears thickness without losing the dreamlike quality.
use_for: Choruses wanting weight while preserving verse texture
Drops where the verse atmosphere should bleed through

advisor_extract.description: Apply modulation effect (phaser, chorus, flanger — slow rate) to clean guitar throughout a section. Creates a continuously moving texture that doesn't rely on note motion.

Cherub_Rock_Siamese_Dream.structural_blueprint: intro (drum solo intro by Jimmy Chamberlin — drum kit alone) -> riff enters (classic Pumpkins layered distortion) -> verse -> chorus -> verse -> chorus -> bridge_solo (extended Corgan lead) -> chorus_outro. Drum-led intro is unusual for grunge — establishes Chamberlin as a featured player.
signature_production_choices: Drum kit recorded with ROOM mics emphasized — Chamberlin's playing has reverberant SIZE
Guitar layers stacked Pumpkins-thick from first riff
Chamberlin's snare hits VERY hard — sustained ringing
Cherub_Rock_Siamese_Dream.rhythmic_signature: Faster tempo than typical Pumpkins. Chamberlin's drums extremely active — fills, double-kick, syncopated snare. He's a JAZZ-trained drummer playing rock.
Cherub_Rock_Siamese_Dream.harmonic_signature: Standard rock chord progression in minor key — straightforward to give the drum + guitar work room.
instrumental_signature: Chamberlin drum kit as featured player (intro)
Cherub_Rock_Siamese_Dream.the_moment_people_remember: the drum intro — Chamberlin alone for 8 bars before the band joins. Establishes the song's rhythmic complexity from the first second.

advisor_extract.description: Open the song with drum kit ALONE for 8 bars (drums as featured intro instrument, not just timekeeper). Rest of band enters after the drummer has established personality.
use_for: Intros that want to feature rhythmic complexity
songs with strong drummers as compositional voice

advisor_extract.description: Use jazz-style drum vocabulary (active fills, ghost notes, broken backbeat, syncopation) WITHIN a rock arrangement. Creates rhythmic interest without losing the genre identity.
use_for: Verse 2 patterns adding density via drum complexity

Tonight_Tonight_Mellon_Collie.structural_blueprint: intro (orchestral strings — full orchestra) -> verse (orchestra continues with rock band entering subtly) -> chorus (full integration: rock band + orchestra TOGETHER) -> verse -> chorus -> bridge_orchestral -> chorus -> orchestral_outro. The orchestra is NOT a backing element — it's an EQUAL partner with the rock band.
signature_production_choices: Full string orchestra arranged by Audrey Riley — not just sweeteners but PRIMARY harmonic content
Rock band (drums, bass, electric guitar) recorded to interact with orchestra, not over it
Drum sound bright and crisp — sits with strings sonically
Vocal mid-mix with reverb — chamber-music feel
Tonight_Tonight_Mellon_Collie.rhythmic_signature: Driving rock tempo (137 BPM) but the orchestra makes it feel less aggressive — 4/4 with anthemic feel.
Tonight_Tonight_Mellon_Collie.harmonic_signature: Major key, but uses a I-V-vi-IV cycle with orchestral voicings that add 7ths and suspensions — sophisticated harmony for rock.
instrumental_signature: full string orchestra as PRIMARY instrument
Tonight_Tonight_Mellon_Collie.the_moment_people_remember: the orchestral arrangement integration — the strings are not 'on top' of the rock band, they ARE the rock band's harmonic foundation. Genre expansion via orchestration.

advisor_extract.description: When using orchestral elements (string section, woodwind), make them EQUAL to band — primary harmonic content, not background lush. Arrange band to interact with orchestra rather than orchestra filling space behind band.
use_for: Bridge sections that depart from rock palette

advisor_extract.description: Standard rock 4/4 at 130-140 BPM, but harmonic content provided primarily by strings (with sus, 7th color tones). Drums + bass + guitar do groove; strings do harmony. Each does what they do best.

1979_Mellon_Collie.structural_blueprint: intro (drum machine + clean guitar) -> verse (electric bass joins, Corgan vocal) -> chorus (slight expansion, no wall-of-distortion) -> verse -> chorus -> bridge (drum machine prominent) -> chorus -> outro. Significantly different palette from typical Pumpkins — drum machine + clean guitars dominate.
signature_production_choices: DRUM MACHINE primary kit (likely Roland) instead of Chamberlin acoustic kit — instantly different palette
Clean electric guitars with chorus modulation — no distortion walls
Vocal less nasal than typical Corgan — softer delivery
Production by Flood and Alan Moulder — they bring electronic palette experience
1979_Mellon_Collie.rhythmic_signature: Drum machine = perfectly mechanical groove. Hi-hat 8ths, kick on 1, snare on 3 (note: not typical 2/4 backbeat — uses the AIC Would?-style stride pattern).
1979_Mellon_Collie.harmonic_signature: Major-key cycle, optimistic feel, simple chord changes that loop.
instrumental_signature: no distorted guitar walls (rare for Pumpkins)
1979_Mellon_Collie.the_moment_people_remember: the drum machine + clean guitar palette — Pumpkins doing electronic-tinged ALT-pop instead of grunge. Showed grunge-era band could expand without losing identity.

advisor_extract.description: Replace acoustic drum kit with drum machine for an entire song or section. INSTANTLY changes the genre/era signature. Mechanical perfection vs human variation = different emotional register.
use_for: Songs/sections wanting departure from rock-band palette
introducing electronic flavor in otherwise organic context

advisor_extract.description: For an artist known for distortion walls, ABSENCE of distortion BECOMES the identity. Same band, same singer, same genre — but no distortion = signal that this song is different.
use_for: songs that want to feel like 'the band other side'

_meta.documentation_sources: Jerry Cantrell interviews, Dave Jerden interviews in Mix Magazine / Tape Op, Greg Prato 'Grunge Is Dead'
_meta.what_distinguishes_them: Vocal harmony architecture — Cantrell + Staley sing CLOSE-INTERVAL harmonies (often a 3rd or perfect 4th apart) with Cantrell carrying the melodic top and Staley below. Drop-tuned guitars (drop D, sometimes drop C) lower the harmonic floor. Slow tempos compared to peers — Sean Kinney drums often behind the beat (drag feel). Dark thematic content matched by minor-key obsessive harmonic minimalism (sometimes 2-3 chord cycles for whole songs).

Them_Bones_Dirt.structural_blueprint: intro_riff (7/8 — instantly destabilizing) -> verse (continues 7/8, vocals enter) -> chorus (RESOLVES to 4/4) -> verse 7/8 -> chorus 4/4 -> bridge_solo -> chorus -> outro. Album opener uses meter shift as 'WAKE UP' device — listener cannot settle.
signature_production_choices: Drum sound dry-but-roomy — Jerden balances between Albini-style room and Wallace-style compression
Guitar layered (Cantrell would track 2-4 takes) — creates wide stereo image of distortion
Vocal harmony recorded with both singers in same room — natural blend, not overdubbed separately
Them_Bones_Dirt.rhythmic_signature: 7/8 grouped 3+2+2 OR 2+2+3 — the riff phrasing makes the grouping clear. Kinney's kick locks the grouping.
Them_Bones_Dirt.harmonic_signature: Drop D root chord cycle — minor key dirgy quality.
instrumental_signature: Cantrell + Staley vocal harmony stack from intro onward
Them_Bones_Dirt.the_moment_people_remember: the 7/8 intro hook — the riff is so memorable that listeners often don't realize they're hearing odd meter until told. The grouping FEELS natural despite being asymmetric.

advisor_extract.description: Open the song (or a major section) in 7/8 (or 5/4 or 5/8) — the asymmetry forces listener attention immediately. Resolve to 4/4 only on chorus.
use_for: Intro that wants to grab attention without volume

advisor_extract.description: When tracking vocal harmony, both singers in same room same take — natural acoustic bleed creates a blend that overdubs cannot replicate. Translates to: in MIDI/sample context, USE THE SAME RAW VOCAL processing chain for harmony lines, no separate mix treatment.
use_for: Vocal harmony arrangements that need to FEEL like one source

Rooster_Dirt.structural_blueprint: intro (acoustic guitar + sparse vocal — almost folk feel) -> verse_quiet (continues clean, vocal exposed) -> chorus_explosion (heavy electric guitar + drums + vocal harmony stack) -> verse -> chorus -> bridge_breakdown -> long_chorus_outro. Slow burn structure — the song GROWS over 6+ minutes via gradual layer addition, not via abrupt dynamic shifts.
signature_production_choices: Acoustic guitar primary in intro/verse, electric heavy in chorus — TWO different guitar PALETTES, not just dynamic shift
Vocal harmony enters AFTER first chorus — withheld for impact
Drum entry delayed — kicks in only when full band is ready
Cantrell wrote about his father's Vietnam experience (lyrical context informs the slow-burn structure)
Rooster_Dirt.rhythmic_signature: Mid-slow tempo. Kinney plays behind the beat in verses (drag feel), locks tighter in chorus.
Rooster_Dirt.harmonic_signature: Modal minor (Aeolian) — repeated 4-chord cycle that doesn't resolve traditionally.
Rooster_Dirt.the_moment_people_remember: the gradual build — the song doesn't HIT, it ACCUMULATES. By the time the full band is playing in extended outro, the listener has been WALKED there.

advisor_extract.description: Use ACOUSTIC guitar (or acoustic-feel clean electric) for verses, ELECTRIC distorted for choruses. The TIMBRE shift is the dynamic — not just volume. Same chord progression can be on both, but the palette change is the song's identity.
use_for: Verse-Chorus dynamic that wants timbral contrast more than dynamic contrast

advisor_extract.description: Vocal harmony is a RESERVED resource. First chorus = single vocal. Second chorus = harmony enters. Adds dimension without changing the score. Listeners notice the 'bigger' second chorus without being able to articulate why.
use_for: Songs with multiple choruses that want each to feel slightly bigger than the last

advisor_extract.description: Drums DON'T enter at the start. Kit comes in only when full band is ready (e.g., bar 8 or chorus 1). When drums enter, they feel inevitable rather than introduced.

Would_Dirt.structural_blueprint: bass intro alone (4 bars — the BASS LINE is the hook) -> guitar enters with simple chord -> vocal enters -> chorus expansion -> verse -> chorus -> outro chorus repeats. Bass-line-as-hook is the song's identity — like Joy Division/post-punk DNA.
signature_production_choices: Bass mixed forward and clean (not distorted, not buried) — Inez's bass line IS the song
Guitar relatively low in mix during bass intro and verse — gives bass space
Cantrell vocal lower in register than typical — broody, almost spoken in verses
Drum entry on chorus is the song's lift moment
Would_Dirt.rhythmic_signature: Steady 4/4, kick on 1, snare on 3 (uncommon — usually 2/4) — gives a STRIDING feel rather than a backbeat groove.
Would_Dirt.harmonic_signature: Drop D guitar over moving bass line — bass defines harmonic motion while guitar holds drone.
instrumental_signature: drop D guitar dropping to subdued role
Would_Dirt.the_moment_people_remember: the bass intro — the way the song STARTS with bass alone for several bars before any other instrument enters. Bass-as-lead is rare in grunge.

advisor_extract.description: Open song with bass playing the central melodic hook ALONE for 4-8 bars. No drums, no guitar. Bass establishes identity. Other instruments enter as ADDITIONS to bass, not replacements.
use_for: Intros that want minimalist hook establishment
songs where bass is the lead instrument

advisor_extract.description: Place snare on beat 3 instead of beats 2 and 4 — creates a 'striding' feel (kick-snare-kick-snare every 2 beats) rather than the standard backbeat. Slower-feeling, doom-adjacent.
use_for: Verse grooves that want broody walk feel

advisor_extract.description: Guitar sustains a single chord/drone, BASS carries the chord changes via root motion. Inverts the typical role (guitar drives chord changes, bass follows root).
use_for: Verse arrangements where guitar wants to be textural

Down_In_A_Hole_Dirt.structural_blueprint: intro (acoustic-style clean electric arpeggios) -> verse (continues clean, vocal harmony from start) -> chorus (electric distortion enters but at modest level) -> verse -> chorus -> bridge_solo -> chorus -> outro. Slow ballad pace. Vocal harmony is FEATURED throughout, not reserved for chorus.
signature_production_choices: Vocal harmony = primary melodic content (Cantrell + Staley locked in close intervals throughout)
Reverb on vocal — gives chamber/cathedral space
Electric guitar in chorus is BARELY heavier than verse — almost a continuous arrangement
Down_In_A_Hole_Dirt.rhythmic_signature: Slow 4/4 ballad. Drums minimal — kick + closed hi-hat + occasional snare on 3.
Down_In_A_Hole_Dirt.harmonic_signature: Aeolian/minor — slow chord changes, sustained voicings.
instrumental_signature: clean electric arpeggio (no distortion in verse)
Down_In_A_Hole_Dirt.the_moment_people_remember: the vocal harmony stack — Cantrell + Staley locked in close 3rds/4ths throughout. The blend of their two voices is the song's identity, and it's been imitated countless times.

advisor_extract.description: Use vocal harmony as PRIMARY melodic content from the song's start — not as a chorus enhancement. Two voices locked in close intervals (3rds, 4ths) become the texture itself.
use_for: Songs that center vocal as the instrument

advisor_extract.description: Strip drums to bare minimum (kick + occasional snare + closed hat) when vocal is the song's focus. Sparse drums = vocal can breathe and harmonies stay audible.

Dirt_Title_Track_Dirt.structural_blueprint: slow heavy intro -> verse_dirgy (vocal forward, riff cycles) -> chorus_lift (slight expansion) -> verse -> chorus -> bridge -> chorus_extended. The whole song stays at SLOW tempo and HEAVY register — no dynamic shifts that release the weight. Punishing slow-burn.
signature_production_choices: Drop D + downtuned half-step (effective drop C# or lower) — extreme low end
Drum hits VERY behind the beat (Kinney drag) — feels like the song is being slowed down by gravity
Vocal lower in register, less harmony than other Dirt tracks — emphasizes the dread
Dirt_Title_Track_Dirt.rhythmic_signature: Slow 4/4 dragged behind beat. Tempo 76 BPM is at the edge of perceivable as 'fast' or 'half-time slow' — lives in a uncomfortable middle.
Dirt_Title_Track_Dirt.harmonic_signature: Modal minor with bVII — repetitive cycle, no real cadence.
Dirt_Title_Track_Dirt.the_moment_people_remember: the unrelenting weight — the song refuses to lift. Even the chorus is heavy. There's no release valve.

advisor_extract.description: Refuse the loud-quiet-loud template entirely. Stay at HIGH density / LOW tempo / HEAVY register throughout. No section provides relief. The unrelenting quality IS the song.
use_for: Songs that want to FEEL like a slow descent
advisor_extract.warning: Risky — needs strong vocal/melodic interest to keep the listener. Without that, it becomes monotonous.

advisor_extract.description: At slow tempo (70-85 BPM), drag drums 15-25ms behind the grid. The drag is more perceivable at slow tempos — feels like gravity on the song.
use_for: Slow heavy sections wanting doom feel

_meta.documentation_sources: Producer interviews in Mix Magazine / Tape Op, Greg Prato 'Grunge Is Dead', Chris Cornell interviews, Matt Cameron drumming clinics
_meta.what_distinguishes_them: Soundgarden uses ODD METERS deliberately as identity — multiple songs in 7/4 or alternating meter. Their drop tunings (drop D, drop C#) lower the harmonic floor below standard. Cornell's multi-octave vocal range is structurally exploited (verse low, chorus high).

Black_Hole_Sun_Superunknown.structural_blueprint: intro (clean watery guitar via Leslie speaker simulation) -> verse (haunting major-key chord cycle, restrained drums) -> chorus (massive expansion, full distortion + Cornell vocal soars) -> verse -> chorus -> bridge (extended atmospheric build) -> chorus_climax_with_outro_solo. Uncanny major-key harmony combined with dark thematic content — the BRIGHTNESS is the unsettling element.
signature_production_choices: Leslie speaker effect on guitar in verses — slow rotary modulation creates underwater/dreamlike feel
Hammond organ doubles guitar in verses (subtle, sits below mix surface) — provides the warm pad layer that distinguishes this from typical grunge
Cornell's vocal recorded close, almost whispered in verse / belted in chorus — extreme dynamic between sections
Drum sound less compressed than Andy Wallace school — more natural, breathing
Black_Hole_Sun_Superunknown.rhythmic_signature: Mid-tempo, kick on 1 and 3 with subtle ghost notes. Matt Cameron uses cymbal swells (not crashes) for transitions — adds atmosphere, not impact.
Black_Hole_Sun_Superunknown.harmonic_signature: Major-7 and add9 chords in verse — bright, slightly dissonant. Modulates to a darker minor area in chorus while STAYING in major key (Mixolydian flavor).
instrumental_signature: Leslie speaker rotary on guitar (or Univox effect)
Cornell's signature high register exploited in chorus
Black_Hole_Sun_Superunknown.the_moment_people_remember: the chorus expansion — when the haunting verse opens up into the soaring chorus. The MAJOR key in the chorus combined with the dark content makes the lift feel sinister rather than triumphant.

advisor_extract.description: Apply slow rotary speaker modulation (or Leslie sim, or slow chorus) to a clean guitar track in verse. Creates underwater/dreamlike texture that reads as 'distance' to the listener.
use_for: Intro / Verse 1 atmosphere that wants warmth without thickness

advisor_extract.description: Place a Hammond organ (or organ-style synth pad) UNDER the guitar in mix — same chord, different timbre, subtle volume. Doubles the harmonic weight without adding density.
use_for: transitioning to chorus while staying in key

advisor_extract.description: Use unmistakably MAJOR-quality harmony (major triads, add9, maj7) for sections with DARK thematic intent. The brightness creates uncanny dissonance with the meaning — feels more sinister than minor would.
use_for: moments where emotional tone should feel 'wrong' on purpose

Spoonman_Superunknown.structural_blueprint: intro (riff in 7/4 establishes meter) -> verse 7/4 (riff continues, vocal enters) -> chorus 4/4 (RESOLVES the meter to standard 4/4 — listener feels relief) -> verse 7/4 -> chorus 4/4 -> bridge with literal SPOON SOLO (real spoons percussion) -> chorus -> outro. Meter shift IS the song's structural identity.
signature_production_choices: Real spoons used as percussion in bridge — recorded as actual instrument, not sample
Drum sound dry and punchy — Matt Cameron's hits are very forward
Guitar in drop D tuning, low end emphasis
Spoonman_Superunknown.rhythmic_signature: 7/4 verse riff is grouped as 4+3 OR 3+2+2 (debated by analysts). Cameron's drumming locks the grouping with kick on 1 and 5 (for 4+3 reading).
Spoonman_Superunknown.harmonic_signature: Modal — minor key with bVII (Aeolian). Repetitive 2-3 chord cycle that's easy to follow despite the meter weirdness.
Spoonman_Superunknown.the_moment_people_remember: the 7/4 → 4/4 transition. The meter resolves at the chorus and the listener feels physical relief — this is the song's emotional payoff, not the chorus content per se.

advisor_extract.description: Verse in 7/4 (or 5/4 or 6/4) — odd meter establishes itself as the 'home'. Chorus REVERTS to 4/4 — listener feels resolution from the meter shift itself. The relief IS the emotional payoff.
use_for: Verse-Chorus dynamic where you want the meter shift to BE the cadence
advisor_extract.warning: Use only if the verse is long enough (8+ bars) to establish 7/4 as a stable feel. Briefly going 7/4 then 4/4 sounds like a mistake.

advisor_extract.description: Replace standard percussion (drum rack hits) with a recorded REAL physical object (spoons, metal pipe struck, kitchen utensil) — adds authenticity and signals that this is a different sonic world for the bridge.
use_for: Bridge moments wanting break from the song's normal palette

Outshined_Badmotorfinger.structural_blueprint: verse 4/4 (slow heavy riff in standard meter) -> chorus 7/4 (the meter SHIFTS for chorus — opposite of Spoonman). The OPPOSITE structural choice from Spoonman: chorus is the unstable meter.
signature_production_choices: Terry Date production — heavier compression than Beinhorn, more 'metal' presence
Drum room mics very wide — kit feels physically large in mix
Cornell vocal slightly distorted on chorus (saturation, not clean delay)
Outshined_Badmotorfinger.rhythmic_signature: 7/4 chorus grouped 4+3, kick on 1 and 5 — same grouping as Spoonman verse, but here it's the destabilizing element instead of the resolution.
Outshined_Badmotorfinger.harmonic_signature: Drop D tuning gives access to D power chord without barre — riff exploits this.
instrumental_signature: drop D guitar with heavy distortion (Mesa Boogie or Ampeg amp likely)
no synths or organ — pure rock
Outshined_Badmotorfinger.the_moment_people_remember: the chorus 7/4 — listener has settled into 4/4 verse, then chorus pulls the rug. Inverse emotional logic from Spoonman.

advisor_extract.description: Inverse of Spoonman: verse in 4/4 (stable), chorus in 7/4 (unstable). Chorus becomes the 'wrong' feeling — destabilizing, off-balance.
use_for: Pre-Drop sections that should feel disorienting

advisor_extract.description: Drop D (or drop C#) tuning lets a single low string deliver root power chord without barring. The riff economy frees up the rest of the hand for ornaments. Fundamental to grunge/metal guitar feel.
use_for: Riff-based verses that need low-end weight

Rusty_Cage_Badmotorfinger.structural_blueprint: fast intro riff -> verse (driving 16th note pattern) -> chorus (different rhythmic feel) -> verse -> chorus -> bridge_breakdown (tempo/feel HALVES — section feels like a different song) -> return to fast original feel -> outro. The bridge breakdown is structurally the most adventurous element.
signature_production_choices: Fast 16th-note kick patterns require precise drum tracking — Cameron's right foot is a metronome
Guitar layered with subtle delay for size
Cornell vocal pushed forward in mix
Rusty_Cage_Badmotorfinger.rhythmic_signature: 16th-note kick pattern in verses (uncommon for grunge, more metal). Bridge breakdown switches to half-time and the drum kit feel is completely different (slower, heavier hits).
Rusty_Cage_Badmotorfinger.the_moment_people_remember: the bridge breakdown — when the song HALVES tempo and turns into a different beast for ~30 seconds, then returns to full speed. The contrast amplifies the return.

advisor_extract.description: Bridge section literally halves the perceived tempo (kick lands every 2 beats instead of every 1, half-time feel). Return to full feel after creates massive momentum.
use_for: Bridge sections that want to feel like a different song

advisor_extract.description: Continuous 16th-note kick (or muted bass equivalent) during verse — creates relentless forward motion. Pairs with palm-muted drop-tuned guitar.

Burden_In_My_Hand_Down_On_The_Upside.structural_blueprint: intro (acoustic-feel clean electric guitar) -> verse (continues clean, vocal exposed) -> chorus (heavy distortion enters, but NOT the wall-of-sound of Badmotorfinger — controlled lift) -> verse -> chorus -> bridge -> chorus. Mature dynamic — the band has learned restraint. The chorus is heavier than the verse but NOT maximally so.
signature_production_choices: Adam Kasper production — more open and airy than Beinhorn or Date
Acoustic-style strumming on electric clean tone — harkens to country/folk influence
Drum compression lighter — rooms breathe
Burden_In_My_Hand_Down_On_The_Upside.rhythmic_signature: Steady 4/4, mid-tempo, simple groove — the song relies on melody/lyric, not rhythmic complexity.
Burden_In_My_Hand_Down_On_The_Upside.harmonic_signature: Major-key folk progression underneath — the SONG sounds folk-rock until the chorus distortion enters.
Burden_In_My_Hand_Down_On_The_Upside.the_moment_people_remember: the controlled dynamic — the band's late-career restraint. The chorus has weight without sacrificing breath. This is the OPPOSITE of SLTS loud-quiet-loud.

advisor_extract.description: Chorus adds distortion but at a CONTROLLED level — not max, not pedal-stomp shock. Maintains breathability. Mature dynamic: chorus is heavier than verse but NOT maximum density.
use_for: Choruses that want lift without overwhelm
Drops in arrangements where Final Drop will be the true peak

advisor_extract.description: Clean electric guitar STRUMMED in acoustic style (large strums, full chords, ringing open strings) — gets a folk-rock feel from a rock instrument.
use_for: Verse arrangements wanting acoustic warmth without acoustic guitar

primary_producers_cited: Steve Albini (In Utero tracking, mostly unmodified)
Scott Litt (In Utero — Heart-Shaped Box and All Apologies remix)
_meta.documentation_sources: Charles R. Cross 'Heavier Than Heaven', Michael Azerrad 'Come As You Are', producer interviews in Sound on Sound / Tape Op / Mix Magazine

Smells_Like_Teen_Spirit_Nevermind.key_area: F minor (with capo / detuning relative to other Nevermind tracks)
Smells_Like_Teen_Spirit_Nevermind.structural_blueprint: intro_riff (4 bars clean) -> verse_quiet (clean guitar + sparse bass + vocal whisper-talk) -> pre_chorus_build -> chorus_explosion (full distortion + vocal doubled + cymbals open) -> verse2_quiet -> chorus -> bridge_solo -> chorus_extended -> outro. The TEMPLATE that defined 'loud-quiet-loud' for a generation.
signature_production_choices: Single distortion pedal stomp (Boss DS-1) at chorus entry — clean→saturation transition is THE moment
Andy Wallace mix added significant compression and high-shelf brightness vs Butch Vig's drier tracking — Cobain reportedly disliked the polish
Drum room very dry, snare very forward in mix
Vocal single-tracked in verse, doubled in chorus (creates the perceived size jump)
Cymbals open WIDE in chorus only — tightly controlled in verse
Smells_Like_Teen_Spirit_Nevermind.rhythmic_signature: kick on 1 and 3, snare backbeat 2/4, hi-hat closed 8ths in verse, open hat + cymbal washes in chorus. Tempo locked, no swing.
Smells_Like_Teen_Spirit_Nevermind.harmonic_signature: alternates between two harmonic centers via 4-chord cycle — verse and chorus use SAME chord progression but with massively different dynamics + timbre. The CONTRAST is the song.
instrumental_signature: Cobain played a left-handed Fender Mustang (modified) — bright, slightly thin tone that sits between bass and lead frequencies
Boss DS-1 distortion pedal as the primary tone shaper
Krist Novoselic bass — warm, rounded, leads the groove
Smells_Like_Teen_Spirit_Nevermind.the_moment_people_remember: the chorus entry — the SECOND the distortion pedal hits at end of pre-chorus. Listeners' bodies physically register the dynamic jump because the verse was so restrained.

advisor_extract.description: Verse uses clean guitar + restrained drums (kick + soft snare only). At chorus entry, ONE single audio event (distortion pedal in original; sample/synth fade-in for our context) flips the WHOLE arrangement to max density.
use_for: chorus entries that need maximum impact

advisor_extract.description: Single vocal in verse, doubled in chorus — the perceived 'bigness' jump comes from layer count, not just volume.
use_for: Pre-Drop to Drop in our structure
